title: 2025/09/08 - GA (A) (3 of 3)
description: 8 September 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee A (2.55pm - 4.55pm) Online Casino Gambling Bill Alexandra Bowling Club Special Olympics Hutt Valley Trust Andrzej Kowalczyk - Secretary Port Nicholson Poneke Cycling Club Nick Warren – President Wanderers Community Sports Club James van der Colk - Manager Wairarapa Multisports Club Inc Catherine Rossiter-Stead – Secretary New Plymouth Riding for the Disabled Kevin Nielsen - President The Salvation Army New Zealand Lisa Campbell - National Operations Manager Paul Barber - Principal Social Policy Analyst Entain New Zealand Limited Sam Moncur - Managing Director Entain NZ Andrew Bouris - CEO Entain ANZ Squash City Invercargill Karyn Flett - Secretary Zoo and Aquarium Association Australasia Nicola Craddock - Executive Director Fitzroy School Suzie Archer – Specialist Teacher Mana Marlins Marsters Swim Club Inc Michelle Warshawsky – Secretary
descriptionSummary: (2.55pm - 4.55pm). Online Casino Gambling Bill. Alexandra Bowling Club

chunks.text: Alright,
Thank you. Welcome back committee members. We've got our first submission after this break is one on Zoom, which is the Alexandra Bowling Club. Who do we have? Um, I'm not sure.
Hi, Erin. Okay.

Just need to turn the camera off. Do you know the, um, so if anyone's here from the Alexandra Bowling Club, if you could just turn your camera on. Okay, fantastic. Thank you. Thank you very much for, um, joining us today. We've got your Russian submission here, and we do have, uh, 10 minutes set aside for your oral submission.

Yep, we can see that. Fantastic. Okay. Thank you. Thank you. First of all, thanks very much for the opportunity to present today. Um, our major concern with the bill is, uh, our communities. So we really feel that there's a need to protect our communities and with the current, um, bill, it doesn't do that as sufficiently as we would like.

So just like to run through this present presentation to talk to that. Thank you. Um, under the current model, um, we rely on funding from the poking machines. So, um, at the moment, poking machines return about 40 to 90% of the revenue back to community groups, which support essential services with within clubs.

However, the online gambling funding model doesn't have the need to donate revenue back to communities like it does, like the poke machines do. And what that means is that as, um, gambling moves away from poke machines into the online, uh, gambling model, um, the amount of funding coming back to our communities will dry up.

So, um, many, many clubs like our own, are, um, totally reliant on the funding. And without that funding, excuse me, I didn't mean to click forward, um, without that funding, um, possibility of our club continuing is pretty remote. Um, the other thing it does is it undermines their social contract.

So in today's world, the, there's a social contract ensuring that gambling profits support the communities that they're harmed for. So our, our local community poker machines are coming back to us. So that is also, um, doesn't happen under this new model. And, and why does our club matter?

I mean, what, what's one less bowling club in the world? Well, we're, we're a bowling club. We're also a, a Paton club. We have Dance Pool, ilead, Mahjong, and cards. So we, we have about 200 members. It's run by three part-time staff members. So we operate on a, on a oily rag.

And the rest of, rest of the, um, the club is run by volunteers. And our club offers a safe space for social interaction and emotional support and a sense of belonging to members. So that is really critical for so isolated individuals. We have a lot of elderly people in our club and our services and what they get from a club is super important.

Um, many of our members participate in physical a activities and the social and, and mental activities, which are just as important. And many of our members are volunteers and they, they, it, it promotes active community and, and involvement and purpose. The other thing that the club does is we're a vital link between members and families.

So we have a lot of elderly people that don't have families around them, and our club provides crucial support connecting our members with their families at times. And for those that don't have families, the club becomes their family. So that is why our club is important. So not just as a Bowls club, it's a social and emotional support network.

Now I wanna tell you about Bob. So Bob, I've changed his name, but this is absolutely true. So Bob, um, has been a vital member of our club for 20 years. Um, he's been responsible for organizing our, our tournaments and he organizes sponsorship and funding, um, for, for the tournaments.

Um, like many others in our club, the club has become an integral part of his life. It connects him to the community. Now, Bob had a mental breakdown, um, a few months ago, and that was because his concern that the club was not gonna continue and his fears, excuse me, his fears are warranted.

Um, the club, as I've mentioned before, will close if we cannot keep continuing to have funding from gambling proceeds. It's that simple operating costs rise and our maintenance costs are never ending. And I, I genuinely feel fear for Bob. Um, if we have to close, I'm now gonna pass you over to my colleague and, and, uh, you can see her on screen there, Helen.

Yeah, Helen lovely. Helen is gonna tell Evelyn's story, um, and I'll just pass my headphones. We had to do it this way. Sorry. No worries. Good afternoon. So I just wanted to speak to you about Evelyn. So Evelyn is actually my mother-in-law.

Um, we've changed her name to keep her private, but Evelyn recently had started, she's actually just recently moved to our area and has always suffered from social anxiety. And over the last few months has actually started coming to our club on the Friday nights, um, and enjoying the social outings.

And for her, this is her only social outing all week. Um, she has started doing some casual bowls, um, and joined in with others, which is something that they all enjoy. But the emotional impact for Evelyn prior to this was that she was really struggling with self-confidence and acceptance in a new community. And so when she comes to the bowling club, like any sports club, she feels very welcome and that she can just be herself If Evelyn has recently put her hand up, um, to help with the club, um, she is developing a renewed sense

of worth, and her smile and laughter is infectious and brings joy to so many others. And if the club was to close, Evelyn would be lost again. And she looks it's something that she looks forward to every Friday night. So this leads on to loneliness. And, um, back in 2023, after the pandemic, the World Health Organization has actually declared loneliness, um, to be a global health, public health concern.

Um, it increases the risk of death by 26 to 29% and increases the risk of heart attack, strokes and dementia. In our aging population since 2018, 42% of New Zealanders have identified as being lonely or feeling lonely. And it's one of the leading causes of people going into residential care in New Zealand.

So by losing community groups and sporting clubs, we are increasing, um, the risk of loneliness for New Zealanders. So Bob and Evelyn are dependent, are depending on you to protect funding streams for a bowling club. Please don't take away their reason for getting outta bed in the morning.

Please ensure that community and sports groups benefit from online gambling proceeds just as much as they do from poking machine funding as they do today. Thank you. Thank you so much for your submission. Uh, I just had a question around about, uh, the costs that you face.

So I've noted in your written submission you said inflation, uh, and sponsorship and also local government support is reducing. And you also noted your, uh, support that you received, uh, over the years through applying for grants and things. Uh, could you just for the committee provide any more detail about that grant element of your funding and how, if, if, or if that's got harder over the last few years?

Um, certainly. So, um, we have done a number of submissions over the years, um, to, we've, um, received the p machine funding from pub charities and, um, a, uh, chair, I can't remember a funding, um, which is from the poke machines. Um, electricity and utilities have been covered at times, um, as well as some, uh, asset purchases. So, um, we were fortunate to get some sponsorship from a retirement home nearby as they built. And we got a van, um, to, uh, to borrow. And we, um, have a, so it's a courtesy van and it takes people to and from bowls, et cetera, and the pub charities was able to fund a carport for that.

So we are able to keep it out, out of the weather. Um, sponsorship is getting harder and harder to get from, from businesses and such. Um, businesses obviously are, are buckling down and have their own concerns with rising expenses, et cetera. It's not an easy time to get funding from businesses as well.

Mm, thank you. Uh, I'll just get you to end your screen share and just see if there's any questions, colleagues, but I think we have almost run out of, um, time now. Anyway, uh, so thank you so much for joining us today and thank you for the amazing work that you do. Um, and Alexandra, it's been, uh, really lovely to hear about that work, um, and appreciate your engagement with the committee today. Thank you.

Thank you for the opportunity. Thank you. Bye. Thank You so much. Thank you. Our next submission is in person. Uh, so we have Andre here from the Special Olympics Hu Valley Trust. I, I guess the crux of the oral mission here today is, is talking about the consequence that the bill could have, uh, for clubs like ourselves if the online gaming, uh, providers are not required to give back to the community.

Um, the same way that, uh, the land base, uh, venues are with, I've read the Martin Report, um, and I think the evidence there is quite clear, um, this report People Sports Collective as online, um, gambling grows. Um, um, it's sort of, it's, it's because it's so easy to pick up your phone, you know, I can pick a phone and start playing right now.

It's, it's, it's cons. You know, you gotta have the land based venues are gonna be able shrink when they shrink the pot of money that's available, um, to, um, register charities like ourselves. Um, we'll, we're starting to fight in a smaller and smaller pool. Um, the briefing paper I think was provided to Pam to, uh, cabinet, um, does not require line operators to give back to the communities.

They argue that communities been us are becoming too dependent on those on class four grants I've written down here. It's misleading, but it's bloody insulting. Um, we are not solely dependent on these grants. We work incredibly hard or our volunteers work incredibly hard to fundraise.

Grants don't replace our effort. They just ease the burden. Um, they allow us to keep our programs running instead of exhausting. Um, our, um, army of volunteers that we need. Also, the cabinet, um, the paper was, I think to, um, presented cabinets contradicts the gambling at 2003.

Uh, crystal clear gambling revenue is meant to benefit, um, community itself. The top that the harmony creates, removing that principle, uh, for online provi, um, providers undermines the entire gambling regulation, in our opinion, think would be foolish to ignore that if online, um, providers don't contribute on the same terms.

Thousands of clubs across New Zealand do struggling. I mean the previous, um, people just sort of, um, added to that. So I think parity is an absolute bottom line in this, um, environment. Um, give me, I'll talk a little bit about our club at the moment. So special in the Hub Valley, we receive no better funding from central or local government.

Uh, not not 1 cent. We have to beg and scrape for everything. Um, every dollar we spend on our program is raised by ourselves. Um, as referenced in my written submission, our annual, um, operating cost is between 60 and 70,000 each year. And that's not to pay for wages, that's not to pay for, um, um, rates or insurances.

We, we, we, we, we run through lean. So everything we have goes towards the, um, hiring of venues when we need them. Um, and also, but in a national summers games year, like we're in now, that, that comes up to 120,000 and plus, when we're sending athletes to in New Zealand, again, we've, we, we have to raise those funds.

Um, the previous submitters talked about that social harm. Mm. Um, our, I mean the program that we run, the Special Olympics program nationally, it saves government money. We keep people of intellectual to save disabilities out of the health system by getting the back of getting them enjoying, um, life.

Um, we could be addressing mental, um, mental health. Um, they talked about the pandemic of loneliness. Well, we, we go some way to in social isolation for a lot of our athletes, especially our older ones, because quite often the only time they get outta the house will do something for themselves is when they come to our, um, uh, our trainings.

Um, they, they also talk about sponsorship. We, we are not, um, if you excuse the term, the sexy option for a lot of people, uh, wanting to sponsor us. Unlike Paralympics who are awesome athletes or elite athletes, we don't get our athletes on TV or, and plus we don't trend very well on social media.

That means we, obviously, we don't provide the banking, but for sponsors on top of that, I treat carefully how I say this, A lot of our athletes aren't exactly what sponsors are looking for. They, they, they can't deliver speeches. A lot of them. Some of them have behavioral issues and behaviors, so they don't, they can't get up there and present, um, for a sponsor.

So that sort of takes a big chunk of available funding away from us. Um, a lot of our athletes, um, they're in supportive care environments. Um, and after they've paid, um, all their living expenses weekly, they're left for about 20 or $30 per week.

So it doesn't sp um, leave a lot of discretionary spending for them to go into, um, paying for sports venues. So while other sports and code may, um, be able to corporate funding or play a fees for Special Olympics, it's just, it's just not a, um, a um, an option for us.

So that leaves us heavily relying on our volunteers. Um, we have a wonderful group of volunteers who coach and manage their sport athletes, um, day in, day out, week in and week out. But too often now we're asking those volunteers to be out there doing sausages, organizing raffles instead of been coaching our athletes on and on, on sports field.

So in fact, most of them now are spending more time fundraising than they are coaching and managing their athletes. And that's not what a lot of them sign up for. And we get volunteer fatigue and a lot, we lose a lot of them. Um, also unlike our, our, um, our neurotypical athlete, we can't, we can't use our athletes to do a lot of fundraising.

They can't go and do firewood drives. They can't, they can't, um, get out there and sell r tickets 'cause it's, it's, it's a challenge for a lot of them, especially those with, um, sensory needs. So that's where we lead. Um, we, um, end up sort of not having the funds available. So when funding dries up, the harsh reality is we will have to reduce programs and or in some cases close.

Um, well, I'm not saying this way because I think there's more than 50 sporting organizations who have made written submissions to this have already said they're on the same predicament without the community funding, that poses massive risk to grassroots sports in this country. Um, now, like I said, special Olympics Heart Valley, we got very proud of our f**k papa that we are the original, um, special Olympics Club in New Zealand.

This is where it all started in the early eighties. Um, obviously my own son, he's a special, he growing confidence into a proud and, um, capable young man 'cause of his special Olympic journey. But I wanna share one story. It's quite po poignant, um, that we've got one athlete, he came to us as 16-year-old.

He was living in supportive care arrangement. He was still living alone in a house with four full-time carers surrounding him. 'cause of his behavior. He, you know, weighed over 150, um, kilos, no exercise. His violent behavior was so serious that the police have, have been caught on on numerous occasions. He's heading to, to the justice system and to the health system.

'cause his health was on the year to collapse. So when he joined us two years ago, um, he's, he's had this positive track in his life. He's, he's dropped the weight. Um, he is, he's playing two sports a week. Um, his behavior's been transformed 'cause he does the exo he can't, uh, x up, he can't participate with us.

And so Special Olympics and it's no exaggeration has changed his life. Um, so the truth is, if our funding disappears, so does this chance to sell on the path, this positive path. He doesn't just disappear with it. He becomes society's problem. The health, um, system cost will be costing it and along with the justice system.

So that's why I, we think parity matters so much on this. If land-based venues are required to get back to the venue, uh, to the community, online providers must do the same on equal terms. Otherwise one industry grows and the rest of us pay the price. I don't what my time is. I Think, I think we use that now. Alright.

'cause um, I think today we've heard from a number of different organizations and it's really, uh, amazing to hear of the incredible work that's going on in the community.

We have about a hundred members currently organized over 20 different events each year, as well as offer training and coaching help.

Um, we aim to make the sport as accessible as possible, providing kit, uh, and equipment if necessary, and keeping the club fees low. Uh, funds help us achieve more than what we can offer just, just from club fees and other methods of fundraising. And we're already sort of noticing that it's a bit harder to come by grants and sponsorship over the last few years. Um, but I'll sort of keep the my and talk just on the effects, um, on our club if that continues and gets worse as this evidence suggests. So grant income is constituting around 10 to 45% of our annual income, depending on how successful we've been with applications during the year.

Uh, recent grants in the last couple of years have helped us to, uh, buy a defibrillator for our club rooms, um, send club rooms on first aid courses, help fund riders traveling to expensive, um, uh, major events around the country, um, by sort of things like a timing system as well as medals and trophies for some of the events we organized.

And, um, as I mentioned, buying kits and equipment that we can loan out to people who don't have their own. Uh, I mean, the, the impact to us of not having these grants to cover these expenses is that we can't do these things or we have to pass on costs to members, um, or put, uh, volunteer resource into fundraising, uh, versus coaching and organizing, all that kind of stuff.

Uh, the sort of the flow on effects, um, further down the line are that there's just extra barriers, uh, that, that, sorry, those previous things would cause extra barriers, um, for people not to join the club or be in the sport. Um, we'd have fewer active members and basically the sport would become harder to access. Um, it's not only these small things though.

Um, in 2020 we finished a multi-year project to resurface the Wellington Velodrome. We raised about 190,000, uh, dollars out of the total 200 acquired just from grants, um, to resurface the Velodrome. Um, the early advice I got was that we wouldn't get this done with sausage sizzles and give a little and raffles and stuff like that.

So yeah, long story story short. So we really couldn't do what we're doing without net grant funding. Uh, I'd be concerned for the club and the sport itself. Um, I'm here obviously, um, to talk about this bill. Um, we understand the objective of it, um, but we believe that it has a critical omission, um, and it's gonna have devastating or likely have devastating impacts across the community sporting sector.

Um, and that includes our organization. Um, so a little bit about Wanderers. We have 750 members. Uh, we started off as a rugby club 74 years ago, 75th next year. Um, we also now have cricket. We have a community gym, um, and we have squash courts.

In the past, we've had a netball, we've had football, um, rugby league, indoor bowls, and a raft of other balls. And we've serviced the, uh, the Brightwater community and surround the communities with basically whatever's needed at the time, and we continue to do so. Um, it allows us to employ staff, which I can assure you is definitely needed now. Um, with, with the increased regulation, um, health and safety, um, and all those sorts of, uh, things that go on in the background that volunteers, uh, don't really think about. Um, we, we, and, and as well, we want our volunteers to be focusing on, on, on the, on field.

So this funding allows us to provide a safe environment for all of our, um, players. Um, it covers facility maintenance and improvements. So our facilities are, um, are of a pretty good standard. Um, and it's, you know, things like being able to put in LED lights. Um, field lights allow our members to use the fields year round at whatever time.

Um, it's covered improvements to our gym, um, and our gym's had a massive impact on the community. So, so just one, um, one community outreach example is we've recently, well, five years ago started a, a, a senior mobility program, which is aimed at, um, getting more older people, so 55 plus into the gym to reduce the chance of them falling over pretty much in the, in the future.

Um, and that started off with five members, and over the last five years, it has grown to 100, um, which takes, which takes a lot of, um, I guess effort to, to keep on top of, of all of the, the varying, um, health conditions that these people have.

So grant funding has allowed us to cover an instructor to, to pay attention to all these health conditions, build rapport with, with these older people. Um, and now, you know, 100 people from our very small region are benefiting from, from the service. Um, without the funding, our membership just couldn't afford to pay the fees, um, associated with, with running a sports club.

So, you know, $250,000 is our, um, our, our annual expenses. So the, the revenue we get from membership fees is only around $30,000. So that is a significant gap, uh, which, you know, which needs to be filled with something. Um, and, and we are fortunate currently that, that we do quite well with our grant funding, um, and we can provide these opportunities.

So for me, the, the fundamental flaw, um, is of, of this current bill is that it would abandon the, the principle that a portion of gambling proceeds must be returned to the community. Um, and by licensing up to the 15 online operators, without their obligation, the bill would create a very unlevel playing field.

So while I know we would be eligible for grant funding going forward, um, it's, it's quite predictable, uh, that a large chunk of that group would, um, would move to online gambling and that the, the pool of funding from the, uh, the traditional poking machines would go down while the online, um, you know, sort of well the, the people going to the online would increase, um, and, and, and then decreasing, uh, funding for nonprofit organizations around the country.

Um, so if it has passed as it is, uh, without an alternative, there will be a financial crisis. As I know you've heard, um, from many nonprofit organizations around the country. Um, it'll be no different to Wanderers without the $110,000 or of a significantly reduced funding pool. Um, you know, we just wouldn't be able to carry on the same way we're going.

And that would lead to pretty much a reverse in all of those positive things. I mentioned before, it would be the degradation of facilities. We wouldn't be able to, um, upgrade to LED lights, um, which have been a, a, a hugely positive investment.

We wouldn't be able to fund these community outreach programs because they would be running at a loss to the club. Um, community events like Maki and all this other stuff that we're doing on the periphery, we just wouldn't be able to do.

Um, we couldn't employ staff. It would be all volunteer run. The operations would be considerably scaled back. Um, and yeah, I guess the, the point here I'm trying to make is that the, the loss to the community would be, um, would, would be great. So our request is that, um, the bill is amended to uphold the principle that ga, that gambling benefits the community.

Um, we urge, um, we urge you to, to establish something requiring these new online casino operators to return a fair share of the, of the New Zealand revenue to the communities from which they profit. Um, the model already exists in, in the old bill or the, the old system. And it would be good to see it apply to this new environment.

Um, you know, regulating this industry shouldn't be at the expense of the, of, of the nonprofit community organizations. Um, thank you for your time. Hopefully that was within, within my timeframe. Thank you. Thanks, Madam Chair, members of the committee, thank you for the opportunity to appear today. Tain welcomes the creation of a legal, well regulated online casino market in New Zealand. Done well. This reform can shift black market activity into a safer, more transparent framework, one that protects consumers and delivers lasting benefits for communities.

Tain already employs more than 400 people across six offices in New Zealand and through the TAB New Zealand contributes over 200 million annually to local racing, sport and community organizations Globally, we operate 35 online casino brands across more than 30 regulated markets in Europe and North America.

That experience tells us regulation can succeed, but only when it is carefully staged and backed by strong enforcement. We are concerned that allocating all 15 licenses immediately risks regulatory failure. Jurisdictions that have moved too quickly such as Netherlands, Brazil, and Ontario, struggled with enforcement.

Too many operators at once means incon, inconsistent harm minimization, weaker consumer protections, and a greater risk of social harm. There is also a real risk of advertising oversaturation. New Zealand's media market has limited capacity to advertising from 15 operators.

We have seen firsthand in both Australia and New Zealand how oversaturation drives public backlash, not just against online casinos, but across the entire gambling sector, including wagering, land-based casinos and the racing industry. We want this reform to succeed.

That's why we recommend Number one, a phased rollout, starting with fewer than 15 licenses. Prioritizing trusted New Zealand based and New Zealand regulated organizations. Two, allowing TAB New Zealand to participate either directly or in partnership with trusted operators to protect the well established model for funding racing, sport and communities.

And three, strengthening enforcement powers, including civil penalties, site blocking the banking tools modeled on Australia's ecma. Can I reiterate that? N 10 brings deep global capability in online casinos. Importantly, we made the deliberate choice to withdraw from New Zealand in 2023 when it was a gray market because our principle is clear, we only operate where gambling is legal and properly regulated from that perspective.

I want to share fi one final observation. If the regulated market here is not commercially viable for responsible operators, the black market will continue to thrive. Customers will inevitably, inevitably be drawn off back to offshore black market operators offering fewer protections, and that creates many unintended consequences, more social harm, less community funding and ero, and an erosion of trust in the system.

That's why success requires two things. First, a viable regulatory framework that allows trusted operators to compete fairly, educate and migrate customers to the regulated sector. And secondly, strong enforcement powers to shut down black market operators who work around and outside the system.

Madam Chair Entertain is committed to working with the committee and the Department of Internal Affairs to ensure the new regime delivers on its promise a safer market, stronger protections, and the sustainable funding to New Zealand communities.
